topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt finality of a gavel strike. My heart gave a sudden, unpleasant lurch. I clicked it open, the subject line a mocking echo of my own submission. The article, the one I had poured weeks of late nights and meticulous research into, was attributed to a complete stranger. A stranger with a name I didn't recognize, a name that belonged to someone else entirely. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, trembling slightly above the keys. I reread the attribution, then the original submission details, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. I started tracing the publication history, my gaze darting across the screen, searching for any anomaly, any misplaced fingerprint. A slow, deliberate breath escaped me, a silent acknowledgment that this was not the end of the line, but the beginning of a complicated investigation.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the glow of the monitor reflecting in my wide eyes. The words swam before me, a betrayal etched in typeface. My stomach clenched, a tight, acidic sensation rising in my throat. It wasn't just a mistake; it felt deliberate, a calculated sleight of hand. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. I began to pull up every correspondence, every draft, trying to reconstruct the timeline, to find the moment the switch had occurred. Every keystroke felt heavy, weighted by suspicion. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my apartment, the floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. I needed to know who had pulled this stunt, and I needed to find out, now.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, a strange stillness washed over me, followed by a sharp, almost painful awareness. I didn't move immediately. Instead, I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles were white, focusing intensely on the pattern of the lettering. The realization dawned slowly, chillingly: my work was being presented as someone else's. A slow burn of irritation began to build, morphing into a focused, almost predatory stillness. I moved to the file folders, my movements precise and economical. Each document was scrutinized, every date cross-referenced. A low murmur escaped my lips, a sound more like grinding stone than speech. I felt a distinct tightening behind my eyes, a need to dissect the deception piece by painstaking piece. The quiet hum of the computer fan seemed deafening in the sudden, charged atmosphere of my workspace.
